Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and 
states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind 
“utility enhancing” outcome? 
As of September 12, 2025 2:27 pm 
 
- 6 -
 
 
 
In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over not only 
how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations 
beyond the problem itself.  
 
 
c) 
Examples of not problem contingent (universal approaches) 
 
As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and 
practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because 
CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be 
able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an 
intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy 
interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA 
approach is not to act.  
 
There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international 
relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state 
preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to 
absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying 
motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal 
institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain state behaviour in general – i.e. 
they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived 
from, their choices about how to manage a s

orks to explain state behaviour in general – i.e. 
they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived 
from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of 
state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a 
particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not 
derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These 
theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go 
address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how 
to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss 
below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument 
that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the 
most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems.  This same dilemma facing 
cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and 
work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on

st”. What is 
important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. the utility 
label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human 
self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales 
 
This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand self-interested 
behaviours at multiple levels of sustainability policy and governance including individual, 
organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global 
governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on 
aggregating individual utility, organizational utility (such as companies returning shareholder value), or 
even states’ utility by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, 
rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars 
within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve 
relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have 
undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on 
some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind 
of beneficial “rational” outcome. 
 
Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is 
motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization. This 
allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and 
nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state 
based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at mu

managing a 
clearly specified “on the ground”

orcing); Optimization (Type 2 reinforcing); 
Compromise (Type 3 reinforcing) and Prioritization (Type 4 reinforcing). This broad treatment that there 
can, and are a number of unique strands – such as “cost benefit analysis” CBA and neo-realist and neo-
liberal IR theories all falling under the “Optimization school”. There is already much work on these 
strands that Cashore’s categories help place, rather replace. The point here is that attention to the 
underlying aspects of these schools – one degree of abstraction up from the strands – allows for greater 
understanding of often unconscious biases of “thought and practice” that unite these strands. There are, to 
be sure, other schools of thought and practice that reinforce each Type – whether exploring the 
environment or for that matter public health or education or social problems. Cashore selected these four 
schools of thought and practice because they, arguably, represent the key forces of unconscious and 
conscious bias that have shaped, and changed, how we trains student and practitioners to view the world, 
bias empirical assessments and solve some problems that make other worse.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
  
To identify these schools of sustainability thought and practice, and leading strands within them Cashore 
disentangled began by first reviewing their approach to utility/non-utility and problem contingent/non-
problem contingent (universal) orientations and then added a number of other dimensions that shed

ovides brief summaries of the cases studied.
5
For those less familiar with QCA, conceptually this may resemble a program output rather than program outcome. QCA,
however, strictly uses the term outcome for “dependent variables” and output for “independent variables.” In this article,
I keep to the QCA terminology.
6
Online Appendix C in Appendix S1 provides an overview of the codes used.
7
See online Appendix A in Appendix S1 for a calibration of the conditions and outcomes.
8
It should be noted that the variance in outcomes observed is skewed toward negative outcomes for O1 and positive out-
comes for O2. The lack of variance in outcomes observed is not expected to affect the theoretical implications of the arti-
cle (i.e. the relevance of including a diffusion of innovations perspective in studying voluntary programs), but it may
affect the generalizability of the empirically observed patterns (Payne & Williams 2005; Schneider & Wagemann 2012).

More concise versions of the club theory were also explored: for example, a model where all rewards were added up, as
well as models where only an individual reward or pair of rewards was included. All of these models capture less variance
in the observed outcomes than the club theory model presented here.
13
Because of space limitations, the full analyses for sufﬁcient conditions for the outcomes O1, O2, ~O1, and ~O2 are pre-
sented in online Appendix A in Appendix S1.

e outcome data for outcome O1 (negative),
but show opposite observations for the stringency of enforcement (present in Case 1; absent in Case 2). It is not
surprising that none of these very simplistic theoretical models explain any of the observed variances in the out-
come data. After all, the literature review pointed out that the outcomes of voluntary programs are likely to be
caused by different interacting conditions (i.e. conjunctural causation).
In step 2 of the iterative process, the full set of conditions from the club theory perspective (Potoski &
Prakash 2009) were explored – that is PR * PE * R1 * R2 * R3 (here “*” indicates the logical AND). While the
club theory perspective captures a considerable part of the variance observed in the second outcome
(0.94 * 19/22 + 0.67 * 3/22 = 90%), its explanatory reach is too limited to capture the variance observed in the
ﬁrst outcome. For O1 there are data conﬂicts like those observed for the individual conditions in step 1.12
In step 3, theoretical models made up of pairs of conditions were then explored, in which the full club theory
was treated as a condition. In this step, two theoretical models capture a part or even the full variance in the out-
come observations. That is, the model that combines club theory with government involvement in the voluntary

example, a solution coverage of 0.50 for outcome O1 indicates that the theoretical model explains 50% of the pos-
itive observations for attracting participants, corresponding to two cases.
The overall variance captured by each model can then be calculated by adding up the various overall solution scores
for the sets of positive and negative observed results for the two outcomes, but correcting their contribution to the full
set of outcomes observed (n = 48; in Eqn (1), this is the sum “c + d + e + f”). Only empirical observations were used for
this process of theory–data matching – thus, the most complex solution from the csQCA analyses.10 Table 3 presents
the results of an iterative process of matching the different theoretical models with the empirical data obtained.
This iterative process commenced with an exploration of how much variance in outcome data was captured
by theoretical models made up of the individual core conditions identiﬁed in the literature review (step 1). For
each core condition, a full csQCA was performed for both outcomes of interest.11 As indicated in Table 3, none
of these theoretical models is capable of capturing the full variance in the outcomes observed. This is a result of
data conﬂicts when looking at the outcome data through the lens provided by a theoretical model made up of an
individual core condition. For example, Cases 1 and 2 show the same outcome data for outcome O1 (negative),
but show opposite observations for the stringency of enforcement (present in Case 1; absent in Case 2). It is not
surprising that none of these very simplisti
